docs.title: NowEDA
docs.text:
Automated Exploratory Data Analysis — built as a native pandas extension with Spark acceleration built in.

NowEDA is a lightweight, modular Python framework that turns any dataset into instant insight. Load any file format, call df.noweda.*, and get a complete EDA report — data quality scoring, PII detection, outlier analysis, correlation mapping, and human-readable insights — with zero boilerplate. Large supported files are automatically accelerated with Spark, and notebook/CLI runs show a loading indicator while work is in progress.

docs.title: Why NowEDA?
docs.text:
Most EDA tools give you charts and tables. NowEDA gives you answers.

Other EDA tools NowEDA "Here are histograms" "Column 'salary' is heavily right-skewed — consider a log transform" Show missing value counts "Column 'email' is 32% missing — imputation recommended" Show correlation matrix "Very strong correlation (0.99) between 'age' and 'salary' — one may be redundant" No security awareness "Column 'email' contains 17 PII email addresses — mask before sharing" Requires specific file format Works with 28 file extensions out of the box

docs.title: Core Design Principles
docs.text:
1. Pandas-native — df.noweda.* feels like part of pandas itself. No wrappers, no new object types to learn.

2. Zero fragile dependencies — NowEDA does not wrap or depend on other EDA libraries (ydata-profiling, Sweetviz, D-Tale). Every analysis is implemented from scratch, so no upstream changes can break your workflow.

3. Plugin-based — Every analysis step is an independent, swappable plugin. Run only what you need, or add your own.

4. Security-aware — Built-in PII and encoding detection that no other EDA tool provides out of the box.

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
import noweda as eda

# Works exactly like pandas — just swap pd.read_csv for eda.read
df = eda.read("transactions.csv")

# All normal pandas operations still work
print(df.head())
print(df.describe())

# Now layer on automated intelligence
print(df.noweda.insights())   # human-readable insight list
print(df.noweda.score())      # data_quality, risk, model_readiness
print(df.noweda.summary())    # raw results from every plugin
report = df.noweda.report()   # everything in one structured dict

Example output:

Insights:
 - Likely identifier column(s) detected: id. Consider excluding from modelling.
 - Column 'email' has high missing rate (32%) — imputation recommended.
 - Very strong correlation (0.99) between 'age' and 'salary'. One may be redundant.
 - PII detected in column 'email': 17 email address(es) found. Mask before sharing.
 - Data quality score is acceptable (77). Minor issues present.
 - Moderate risk level (25). Review PII and encoded columns before sharing.

Scores:
{'data_quality': 77, 'risk': 25, 'model_readiness': 53}

docs.title: Supported Formats
docs.text:
NowEDA reads 28 file extensions across all major tabular data formats:

Category Extensions Delimited text .csv .tsv .tab .txt Spreadsheets .xlsx .xls .xlsm .xlsb .ods .odf .odt JSON .json .jsonl XML .xml HTML .html .htm Columnar (pyarrow) .parquet .feather .orc HDF5 (tables) .h5 .hdf .hdf5 Statistical software .dta .sas7bdat .xpt .sav .zsav Python pickle .pkl .pickle

docs.title: Built-in Plugins
docs.text: Plugin What it analyzes Schema Column roles — id, categorical, numeric, datetime, text Stats Mean, median, std, skewness, min/max, top values Missing Per-column missing rate Duplicates Duplicate rows, constant (zero-variance) columns Correlation Pearson correlation matrix for numeric columns Outliers IQR-based outlier count per numeric column PII Email address detection (extensible) Encoding Base64 and obfuscation signal detection

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install noweda

Spark acceleration is included in the standard install.

For additional format support:

pip install "noweda[parquet]"   # Parquet, Feather, ORC
pip install "noweda[hdf]"       # HDF5
pip install "noweda[spss]"      # SPSS
pip install "noweda[full]"      # Everything

→ Full installation guide

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
noweda data.csv
noweda data.csv --html report.html --json report.json

→ CLI reference

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for all public functions and classes in NowEDA.

docs.title: eda.read() / noweda.read()
docs.text:
import noweda as eda

eda.read(file_path, **kwargs) → pandas.DataFrame

Load any supported file into a pandas DataFrame.

Parameters:

Parameter Type Description file_path str Path to the data file **kwargs any Forwarded to the underlying pandas reader

Returns: pandas.DataFrame

Raises: - ValueError — Unsupported file extension - FileNotFoundError — File does not exist - ImportError — Optional dependency not installed (Parquet, HDF5, SPSS)

Examples:

df = eda.read("data.csv")
df = eda.read("data.xlsx", sheet_name="Q1")
df = eda.read("data.csv", nrows=500, encoding="latin-1")

Large Spark-friendly files are routed through Spark automatically when possible, and the notebook/CLI UI shows a loading indicator while work is in progress.

docs.title: NowEDAAccessor
docs.text:
Registered as df.noweda on every pandas.DataFrame after import noweda as eda.

import noweda as eda
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({"x": [1, 2, 3]})
df.noweda    # → NowEDAAccessor instance

docs.title: df.noweda.insights()
docs.text:
df.noweda.insights() → List[str]

Returns a list of human-readable, actionable insight strings.

Analysis runs on first call and is cached. Subsequent calls return immediately.

docs.title: df.noweda.score()
docs.text:
df.noweda.score() → Dict[str, int | float]

Returns:

{
    "data_quality":    int,   # 0–100
    "risk":            int,   # 0+
    "model_readiness": int,   # 0–100
}

docs.title: df.noweda.summary()
docs.text:
df.noweda.summary() → Dict[str, Any]

Returns raw plugin results:

{
    "schema":     Dict,
    "stats":      Dict,
    "missing":    Dict,
    "duplicates": Dict,
    "correlation":Dict,
    "outliers":   Dict,
    "pii":        Dict,
    "encoding":   Dict,
}

docs.title: df.noweda.report()
docs.text:
df.noweda.report() → Dict[str, Any]

Returns the complete report:

{
    "results":  Dict,   # same as summary()
    "scores":   Dict,   # same as score()
    "insights": List,   # same as insights()
}

docs.title: df.noweda.statsall()
docs.text:
df.noweda.statsall() → None

Prints a rich, fully-formatted analysis report to the terminal or notebook. Combines everything in one call:

  • Scores — data_quality, model_readiness, risk (colour-coded)
  • Column overview — dtype, inferred role, unique count, missing count per column
  • Numeric statistics — count, mean, std, min, 25%, median, 75%, max, skewness (highlighted when |skew| > 1)
  • Categorical statistics — count, unique values, top value, top frequency
  • Insights — full human-readable list
  • Plugin summary — raw output from outliers, duplicates, PII, encoding plugins

Returns None; output is printed directly (suitable for notebooks and terminals).

docs.title: df.noweda.vizall()
docs.text:
df.noweda.vizall() → None

Auto-renders the best visualizations for your dataset based on column types:

Chart Condition Histogram + KDE overlay Every numeric column Bar chart (top 15) Every categorical column with ≤ 30 unique values Correlation heatmap When ≥ 2 numeric columns exist Missing value bar chart When any column has missing values Time-series line plot When datetime + numeric columns both exist

Requires matplotlib (pip install matplotlib). KDE overlay additionally requires scipy.

Returns None; charts are rendered inline (Jupyter) or displayed in a window (terminal).

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: All notable changes to NowEDA are documented here.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• Spark-backed loading is now part of the standard install, with automatic routing for large supported files
  • read_chunked() now uses Spark automatically for large CSV/JSON files when appropriate
  • Notebook, CLI, and accessor methods show a loading indicator while analysis is running
  • GitHub Actions now publishes to PyPI automatically when the package version in pyproject.toml is new

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
Core - noweda.read() — unified file loading API supporting 28 file extensions - df.noweda — pandas accessor for zero-boilerplate EDA - AutoEDAEngine — modular engine orchestrating plugins → scorer → insights - Lazy evaluation with per-accessor result caching

Format support - .csv, .tsv, .tab, .txt — delimited text (auto-detects tab separator) - .xlsx, .xls, .xlsm, .xlsb, .ods, .odf, .odt — spreadsheets - .json, .jsonl — JSON and JSON Lines - .xml — XML - .html, .htm — HTML tables - .parquet, .feather, .orc — columnar formats (optional: pip install "noweda[parquet]") - .h5, .hdf, .hdf5 — HDF5 (optional: pip install "noweda[hdf]") - .dta — Stata - .sas7bdat, .xpt — SAS - .sav, .zsav — SPSS (optional: pip install "noweda[spss]") - .pkl, .pickle — Python pickle - File extension detection is case-insensitive (.CSV works the same as .csv)

Plugins (8 built-in) - SchemaPlugin — column role inference (id, categorical, numeric, datetime, text) - StatsPlugin — descriptive statistics including skewness - MissingDataPlugin — per-column missing rates - DuplicatesPlugin — duplicate rows and constant columns - CorrelationPlugin — Pearson correlation matrix - OutlierPlugin — IQR-based outlier detection - PIIDetectorPlugin — email address detection - EncodingDetectionPlugin — Base64 encoding signal detection

Scoring - data_quality score (0–100) - risk score (0+) - model_readiness score (0–100)

Insights - 9 insight categories covering all plugin outputs and scores - Human-readable, actionable text for every finding

Reporting - Dark-themed, self-contained HTML report with score cards, tables, and visual bars - JSON export - CLI: noweda <file> [--html OUTPUT] [--json OUTPUT]

Error handling - Extension check before file existence check — unsupported formats get a clear error listing valid options - Missing optional dependency errors include the exact pip install command

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: NowEDA ships with a command-line tool installed as noweda. After pip install noweda, you can run analysis directly from your terminal without writing any Python.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
noweda <file>

Reads the file, runs all plugins, and prints a rich report to the terminal — including row/column counts, dtypes, descriptive statistics, and actionable insights.

While the file is loading and the report is being built, the CLI shows a small live status indicator so it is clear what NowEDA is doing.

docs.title: Commands and Options
docs.text:
noweda <file> [--html OUTPUT] [--json OUTPUT]

Arguments:
  file              Path to the dataset file (any supported format)

Options:
  --html OUTPUT     Export an HTML report to OUTPUT path
  --json OUTPUT     Export a JSON report to OUTPUT path
  --help            Show this message and exit

docs.title: Redirecting Output
docs.text:
The terminal output is plain text and can be piped or redirected:

# Save terminal output to a text file
noweda data.csv > analysis.txt

# Pipe insights through grep
noweda data.csv | grep "PII"
noweda data.csv | grep "missing"

docs.title: Using the CLI in Scripts
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash

# Example: automated daily data quality check
FILE="daily_export.csv"
REPORT="reports/$(date +%Y-%m-%d).html"

echo "Running NowEDA on $FILE..."
noweda "$FILE" --html "$REPORT" --json "${REPORT%.html}.json"
echo "Report saved to $REPORT"

docs.title: Installing the CLI
docs.text:
The CLI is installed automatically with the package:

pip install noweda
noweda --help

If noweda is not found after installing, your Python Scripts directory may not be on your PATH. Try:

python -m noweda.cli data.csv

Or activate your virtual environment first:

source .venv/bin/activate
noweda data.csv

docs.title: General
docs.text:
Q: Does NowEDA modify my DataFrame?

No. NowEDA only reads your DataFrame. Plugins never mutate the input. The accessor stores analysis results on its own instance, not on the DataFrame object.

Q: Does NowEDA work with DataFrames I didn't load with eda.read()? (e.g., pd.read_sql, pd.read_csv)

Yes. As long as import noweda as eda has been executed, df.noweda.* is available on any pandas DataFrame, regardless of how it was created.

import noweda as eda
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_sql("SELECT * FROM orders", conn)
df.noweda.insights()   # works

Q: How do I use NowEDA with a DataFrame from a database?

import noweda as eda
import pandas as pd
import sqlite3

conn = sqlite3.connect("mydb.sqlite")
df = pd.read_sql("SELECT * FROM transactions LIMIT 10000", conn)
df.noweda.score()

Q: Why is df.noweda not available?

You need to import noweda as eda in the same Python session. The import is what registers the accessor. If you just did from noweda.plugins.pii import PIIDetectorPlugin without importing the top-level package, the accessor won't be registered.

import noweda as eda   # this line is required

Q: I updated a column and re-ran df.noweda.insights() — it returned the same old results. Why?

The accessor caches results after the first call. To re-run analysis on modified data, either:

  1. Run analysis on the modified DataFrame as a new object: 

    df["col"] = df["col"].fillna(0)
df.noweda.score()   # stale — used original df

df2 = df.copy()
df2.noweda.score()  # fresh — new accessor instance

  2. Or call engine.run_df() directly without caching: 

    from noweda.core.engine import AutoEDAEngine
from noweda.plugins import default_plugins

engine = AutoEDAEngine(default_plugins())
report = engine.run_df(df)   # always fresh, no cache

docs.title: Formats
docs.text:
Q: My file has a .txt extension but it's tab-separated. How do I load it?

df = eda.read("data.txt", sep="\t")

.txt is treated as CSV by default. Pass sep= to override.

Q: How does NowEDA handle very large files?

eda.read() automatically uses Spark for large supported files, and eda.read_chunked() can also use Spark when chunked loading makes sense. You still get pandas DataFrames back, but the load step is faster and the UI shows progress while the work is running.

Q: I have a multi-sheet Excel file. How do I load a specific sheet?

df = eda.read("report.xlsx", sheet_name="Q3 Sales")

All **kwargs are passed to pd.read_excel().

Q: Can NowEDA read files from a URL?

Not directly — eda.read() / noweda.read() requires a local file path. Download first:

import urllib.request
urllib.request.urlretrieve("https://example.com/data.csv", "data.csv")
df = eda.read("data.csv")

Q: I get ImportError: Reading .parquet files requires 'pyarrow'. How do I fix it?

pip install "noweda[parquet]"

docs.title: Plugins & Analysis
docs.text:
Q: How do I run only specific plugins?

from noweda.core.engine import AutoEDAEngine
from noweda.plugins.missing import MissingDataPlugin
from noweda.plugins.pii import PIIDetectorPlugin

engine = AutoEDAEngine([MissingDataPlugin(), PIIDetectorPlugin()])
report = engine.run_df(df)

Q: My DataFrame has no numeric columns and correlation returns {}. Is that a bug?

No. The Correlation plugin only runs on numeric columns. With no numeric data, there's nothing to correlate.

Q: The Schema plugin marked my age column as id_candidate. Why?

id_candidate for integer columns requires 100% uniqueness. If your age column has all distinct values (no repeated ages), it triggers this rule. In practice, adding more rows usually causes some ages to repeat and removes the flag. You can also just ignore this insight for domain columns you know are not IDs.

Q: The PII plugin didn't flag my phone numbers. Why?

The built-in PII plugin only detects email addresses. Phone detection requires a custom plugin — see Writing Custom Plugins and the Security Audit Example.

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text:
Q: My model_readiness score is very low even though my data looks clean. Why?

Common causes: - Heavily skewed columns (|skewness| > 2) — each one reduces readiness by 5 - Many text-role columns (free text needs NLP preprocessing) — each reduces by 3 - Missing values — even small amounts reduce readiness

Check df.noweda.summary()["stats"] for skewness values and df.noweda.summary()["schema"] for column roles.

Q: How do I interpret a risk score of 25?

Risk Level 0 No risk signals 1–20 Low 21–50 Moderate >50 High

A risk of 25 means one PII column (e.g., emails, +15) and one encoded column (+10) were detected. Check df.noweda.summary()["pii"] and ["encoding"] to see exactly what was found.

docs.title: Reports
docs.text:
Q: Can I share the HTML report with non-technical stakeholders?

Yes. The HTML report is fully self-contained — no JavaScript, no external CSS, no server needed. Email the .html file directly.

Q: Can I customise the HTML report colours or layout?

Yes. The HTML generator is in noweda/report/html.py. All CSS is inline. Modify the CSS variables at the top of the template or add/remove sections as needed.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
Q: How do I contribute a new plugin?

  1. Create your plugin file in noweda/plugins/
  2. Inherit from BasePlugin, set a unique name, implement run(df)
  3. Add it to tests/
  4. Open a pull request at github.com/codewithdaniel1/NowEDA

docs.title: HTML Reports
docs.text: NowEDA can export a self-contained, dark-themed HTML report — no server required, no internet connection needed. Open it in any browser and share it with anyone.

docs.title: From Python
docs.text:
from noweda.report.html import generate_html_report

report = df.noweda.report()
generate_html_report(report, "report.html")

docs.title: From the CLI
docs.text: noweda data.csv --html report.html

docs.title: Score Cards
docs.text:
Three prominent score cards at the top of the report:

Card What it shows Data Quality Score out of 100, colour-coded green/yellow/red Risk Level Risk score, colour-coded (low=green, high=red) Model Readiness Score out of 100, colour-coded

docs.title: Insights
docs.text: A dark-card list of every insight generated by NowEDA, ordered by category. Each insight is highlighted with a purple left-border accent.

docs.title: Column Schema Table
docs.text:
A table listing every column with:

  • Column name
  • Dtype (e.g., int64, object, float64)
  • Role badge — colour-coded by type (purple=ID, cyan=categorical, green=numeric, lilac=datetime, grey=text)
  • Unique values count
  • Uniqueness ratio (unique / total rows)

docs.title: Missing Values
docs.text:
A table showing the missing rate for each column, with an inline colour-coded bar chart:

  • Red bar — >30% missing
  • Yellow bar — >0% missing
  • Green bar — 0% missing

docs.title: Duplicates & Constants
docs.text:
A two-row summary table:

  • Duplicate rows count and percentage
  • List of constant (zero-variance) columns, or "None"

docs.title: Outliers
docs.text: A table of numeric columns that have at least one outlier, sorted by outlier count descending.

docs.title: PII Detection (highlighted in red)
docs.text: When PII is detected, this section appears with a red border to make it visually prominent. Lists each column and the findings.

docs.title: Encoding Detection (highlighted in yellow)
docs.text: When encoded columns are detected, this section appears with a yellow border. Lists each column and the detected encoding type.

docs.title: Footer
docs.text: Reports the NowEDA branding and the generation timestamp.

docs.title: Customising the Report
docs.text:
The HTML generator lives in noweda/report/html.py. All styling is inline CSS — no external assets, making the file fully self-contained and portable.

To customise colours, add sections, or change the layout, modify html.py or subclass/replace generate_html_report.

docs.title: Example: add a custom section
docs.text:
from noweda.report.html import generate_html_report as _base

def my_generate_html_report(report, output_path, custom_notes=""):
    # Generate the base report first (writes the file)
    _base(report, output_path)

    # Inject a custom section before </body>
    with open(output_path, "r") as f:
        html = f.read()

    custom_section = f"""
    <div class="section">
        <h2>Custom Notes</h2>
        <p>{custom_notes}</p>
    </div>
    """
    html = html.replace("</div>\n</body>", f"{custom_section}</div>\n</body>")

    with open(output_path, "w") as f:
        f.write(html)

docs.title: Sharing Reports
docs.text:
HTML reports are fully self-contained — all CSS is inline, no JavaScript, no external fonts. To share:

  1. Send the .html file directly (email, Slack, etc.)
  2. Upload to any static file host (S3, Google Drive, Dropbox)
  3. Serve from any web server

The file will render identically in any modern browser without any dependencies.

docs.title: Insights Engine
docs.text: The Insights Engine translates raw plugin output and scores into plain-English, actionable statements. Instead of reading tables of numbers, you get a direct list of what matters and what to do about it.

docs.title: Getting Insights
docs.text:
insights = df.noweda.insights()
# Returns: List[str]

for insight in insights:
    print(insight)

docs.title: Insight Categories
docs.text: Insights are generated in this order:

docs.title: 1. Schema Insights
docs.text:
Generated by the Schema plugin results.

Trigger Insight One or more id_candidate columns "Likely identifier column(s) detected: [cols]. Consider excluding from modelling." One or more categorical or categorical_numeric columns "Column(s) with low cardinality (likely categorical): [cols]." One or more datetime columns "Datetime column(s) detected: [cols]. Temporal features may be valuable."

docs.title: 2. Missing Value Insights
docs.text:
Generated per column, based on missing rate.

Missing rate Insight > 50% "Column '[col]' is X% missing — consider dropping it." 30–50% "Column '[col]' has high missing rate (X%) — imputation recommended." 0–30% "Minor missing values in: '[col1]', '[col2]', ..." (grouped)

docs.title: 3. Duplicate and Constant Column Insights
docs.text: Trigger Insight Any duplicate rows "X duplicate row(s) detected (Y% of data). De-duplication is recommended." Any constant columns "Constant (zero-variance) column(s) detected: [cols]. These carry no information and can be dropped."

docs.title: 4. Skewness Insights
docs.text:
Generated from stats plugin results, per numeric column.

Skewness Insight > 2 (right-skewed) "Column '[col]' is heavily right-skewed (skewness=X.XX). Log or power transform may help." < -2 (left-skewed) "Column '[col]' is heavily left-skewed (skewness=X.XX). Log or power transform may help."

docs.title: 5. Outlier Insights
docs.text:
Generated per numeric column with at least one outlier.

Trigger Insight Any outliers "Column '[col]' has X outlier(s) (IQR method). Review before modelling."

docs.title: 6. Correlation Insights
docs.text:
Generated for each unique pair of numeric columns.

Absolute correlation Insight > 0.9 "Very strong correlation (X.XX) between '[col1]' and '[col2]'. One may be redundant." 0.7 – 0.9 "Strong correlation (X.XX) between '[col1]' and '[col2]'."

Each pair is reported only once.

docs.title: 7. PII Insights
docs.text:
Generated per column with PII detected.

Trigger Insight Any PII column "PII detected in column '[col]': X email address(es) found. Mask or remove before sharing."

docs.title: 8. Encoding Insights
docs.text:
Generated per column with encoding signals.

Trigger Insight Any encoding detected "Column '[col]' may contain encoded data (possible_base64). Inspect for hidden payloads or obfuscation."

docs.title: 9. Overall Score Insights
docs.text:
Generated from the scores.

Data quality:

Score Insight ≥ 90 "Data quality score is excellent (≥90). Dataset looks clean." 70 – 89 "Data quality score is acceptable (X). Minor issues present." < 70 "Data quality score is low (X). Significant cleaning required."

Risk:

Risk Insight 0 "No risk signals detected." 1 – 20 "Low risk level (X). Some sensitive indicators found." 21 – 50 "Moderate risk level (X). Review PII and encoded columns before sharing." > 50 "High risk level (X). Sensitive data likely present — handle with care."

docs.title: Filtering Insights
docs.text:
You can filter insights by keyword to focus on what matters for your use case:

insights = df.noweda.insights()

# PII-related only
pii_insights = [i for i in insights if "PII" in i or "email" in i.lower()]

# Missing-data related only
missing_insights = [i for i in insights if "missing" in i.lower() or "dropping" in i.lower()]

# Correlation-related only
corr_insights = [i for i in insights if "correlation" in i.lower()]

# Risk-related only
risk_insights = [i for i in insights if "risk" in i.lower() or "sensitive" in i.lower()]

docs.title: Insights Source
docs.text: The insight generation logic lives in noweda/insights/generator.py. To add custom insight rules (e.g., business-domain checks), subclass InsightGenerator.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.8 or higher
  • pip

NowEDA's core dependencies are installed automatically:

Package Version Purpose pandas ≥ 1.3 DataFrame engine numpy ≥ 1.21 Numeric computation pyspark ≥ 3.4 Spark-backed ingestion for large files openpyxl ≥ 3.0 Excel (.xlsx) reading lxml ≥ 4.6 XML and HTML parsing

docs.title: Standard Install
docs.text:
pip install noweda

This gives you full support for: CSV, TSV, Excel, JSON, XML, HTML, Stata, SAS, Pickle, and Spark-backed large-file loading.

docs.title: Install with Optional Format Support
docs.text:
Some formats require additional libraries. Install them as extras:

Parquet / Feather / ORCHDF5SPSSEverything 
pip install "noweda[parquet]"

Installs pyarrow. Enables .parquet, .feather, .orc.

pip install "noweda[hdf]"

Installs tables (PyTables). Enables .h5, .hdf, .hdf5.

pip install "noweda[spss]"

Installs pyreadstat. Enables .sav, .zsav.

pip install "noweda[full]"

Installs all optional dependencies at once.

docs.title: Install from Source (Development)
docs.text:
If you want to contribute or run from the latest code:

git clone https://github.com/codewithdaniel1/NowEDA.git
cd NowEDA

# Create a virtual environment (recommended)
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate      # macOS/Linux
.venv\Scripts\activate         # Windows

# Install in editable mode
pip install -e .

# Install dev tools
pip install pytest mkdocs mkdocs-material

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
import noweda as eda
print(eda.__version__)   # 0.1.0

import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame({"x": [1, 2, 3]})
print(df.noweda.score())    # {'data_quality': 100, 'risk': 0, 'model_readiness': 97}

docs.title: What If an Optional Dependency Is Missing?
docs.text:
NowEDA gives you a clear, actionable error instead of a confusing traceback:

eda.read("data.parquet")
# ImportError: Reading .parquet files requires 'pyarrow'.
# Install it with:  pip install noweda[parquet]

docs.text: pip install --upgrade noweda

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get up and running in under 5 minutes.

docs.title: Step 1 — Install
docs.text: pip install noweda

docs.title: Step 2 — Load your data
docs.text:
eda.read() replaces every pd.read_* call. The file format is detected automatically from the extension.

import noweda as eda

df = eda.read("data.csv")
df = eda.read("data.xlsx")
df = eda.read("data.json")
df = eda.read("data.parquet")  # requires: pip install "noweda[parquet]"

**kwargs are forwarded directly to the underlying pandas reader:

df = eda.read("data.xlsx", sheet_name="Sales Q1")
df = eda.read("data.csv", nrows=1000, encoding="latin-1")

For very large Spark-friendly files, eda.read() can switch to PySpark automatically. Spark is bundled with NowEDA, so there is no extra install flag or backend setting.

The returned object is a standard pandas DataFrame — every pandas method still works:

df.head()
df.describe()
df["column"].value_counts()
df.groupby("category").mean()

docs.title: Step 3 — Run EDA
docs.text: The df.noweda accessor is available on every DataFrame after importing noweda.

docs.title: Get human-readable insights
docs.text:
for insight in df.noweda.insights():
    print(insight)

Likely identifier column(s) detected: id. Consider excluding from modelling.
Column 'email' has high missing rate (32%) — imputation recommended.
Very strong correlation (0.99) between 'age' and 'salary'. One may be redundant.
PII detected in column 'email': 17 email address(es) found. Mask before sharing.
Data quality score is acceptable (77). Minor issues present.
Moderate risk level (25). Review PII and encoded columns before sharing.

docs.title: Get scores
docs.text:
print(df.noweda.score())

{
    'data_quality': 77,    # 0–100, higher = cleaner
    'risk': 25,            # 0+, higher = more sensitive data present
    'model_readiness': 53  # 0–100, higher = more ready for ML
}

docs.title: Get the full plugin results
docs.text:
summary = df.noweda.summary()

summary["schema"]       # column roles (id, numeric, categorical, datetime, text)
summary["stats"]        # descriptive statistics per column
summary["missing"]      # per-column missing rates
summary["duplicates"]   # duplicate rows, constant columns
summary["correlation"]  # Pearson correlation matrix
summary["outliers"]     # IQR-based outlier counts
summary["pii"]          # PII findings
summary["encoding"]     # encoding detection results

docs.title: Get everything at once
docs.text:
report = df.noweda.report()
# report["results"]  → all plugin outputs
# report["scores"]   → quality, risk, readiness
# report["insights"] → human-readable list

docs.title: HTML report (shareable, dark-themed)
docs.text:
from noweda.report.html import generate_html_report

report = df.noweda.report()
generate_html_report(report, "my_report.html")

Or via CLI:

noweda data.csv --html my_report.html

docs.title: JSON export
docs.text: noweda data.csv --json my_report.json

docs.title: Step 5 — Use selectively (run specific plugins only)
docs.text:
You don't have to run all plugins. Compose exactly what you need:

from noweda.core.engine import AutoEDAEngine
from noweda.plugins.missing import MissingDataPlugin
from noweda.plugins.pii import PIIDetectorPlugin

engine = AutoEDAEngine([MissingDataPlugin(), PIIDetectorPlugin()])
report = engine.run_df(df)

print(report["results"]["missing"])
print(report["results"]["pii"])

docs.title: What's Next?
docs.text:
• Reading Data — all 28 supported formats
  • The NowEDA Accessor — full df.noweda.* API
  • Plugin Reference — what each plugin detects and how
  • Scoring System — how scores are calculated
  • Writing Custom Plugins — extend NowEDA for your use case

docs.title: Scoring System
docs.text: NowEDA produces three scores for every dataset. Scores give you a fast, quantified summary of data quality, risk exposure, and machine learning readiness.

docs.title: The Three Scores
docs.text:
scores = df.noweda.score()
# {'data_quality': 77, 'risk': 25, 'model_readiness': 53}
Score Range Higher means data_quality 0 – 100 Cleaner, more complete data risk 0 – ∞ More sensitive or obfuscated data detected model_readiness 0 – 100 More ready for machine learning without preprocessing

docs.title: data_quality — How It's Calculated
docs.text:
Starts at 100 and is penalised by data quality issues.

Issue Condition Penalty Missing values (critical) >50% missing per column −10 per column Missing values (high) 30–50% missing per column −5 per column Missing values (low) >0–30% missing per column −2 per column Duplicate rows (many) >10% of rows are duplicates −10 Duplicate rows (some) >0% of rows are duplicates −3 Constant columns Each zero-variance column −3 per column Outliers (many) >50 total outliers across all columns −10 Outliers (some) >10 total outliers across all columns −5

Final score is clamped to [0, 100].

Example:

Starting score:                   100
Column 'encoded_field' >50% null: -10
Column 'email' 30-50% null:        -5
Minor nulls in 3 columns:          -6
3 outliers in 'salary':             0  (total ≤10, no penalty)
─────────────────────────────────────
data_quality:                       79

docs.title: risk — How It's Calculated
docs.text:
Starts at 0 and is increased by security risk signals.

Issue Condition Risk added PII column Each column with PII detected +15 Encoded column Each column with encoding detected +10

There is no upper bound on risk. A dataset with 5 PII columns and 3 encoded columns gets risk = 5×15 + 3×10 = 105.

Risk interpretation:

Risk value Level Recommendation 0 None Safe to share 1 – 20 Low Review findings before sharing 21 – 50 Moderate Redact or mask identified columns > 50 High Do not share without thorough review

docs.title: model_readiness — How It's Calculated
docs.text:
Starts at 100 and is penalised by anything that would require preprocessing before training a model.

Issue Condition Penalty Missing values (critical) >50% missing per column −15 per column Missing values (high) 30–50% missing per column −8 per column Missing values (low) >0–30% missing per column −3 per column Constant columns Each zero-variance column −5 per column Heavy skew Each column with |skewness| > 2 −5 per column Outliers (many) >50 total outliers −10 Outliers (some) >10 total outliers −5 Text/unknown columns Each column with role text or unknown −3 per column

Final score is clamped to [0, 100].

docs.title: Reading Score Insights
docs.text:
The insight generator adds human-readable score summaries:

data_quality Insight ≥ 90 "Data quality score is excellent (≥90). Dataset looks clean." 70 – 89 "Data quality score is acceptable (X). Minor issues present." < 70 "Data quality score is low (X). Significant cleaning required." risk Insight 0 "No risk signals detected." 1 – 20 "Low risk level (X). Some sensitive indicators found." 21 – 50 "Moderate risk level (X). Review PII and encoded columns before sharing." > 50 "High risk level (X). Sensitive data likely present — handle with care."

docs.title: Accessing Scores in Code
docs.text:
scores = df.noweda.score()

# Gate on quality
if scores["data_quality"] < 70:
    print("Data needs cleaning before use.")

# Gate on risk
if scores["risk"] > 0:
    print("Sensitive data detected — review before sharing.")

# Gate on model readiness
if scores["model_readiness"] >= 80:
    print("Dataset is ML-ready.")
else:
    print(f"Model readiness: {scores['model_readiness']} — preprocessing needed.")

docs.title: Scorer Source
docs.text: The scoring logic lives in noweda/scoring/scorer.py. To customise scoring for your domain (different thresholds, additional penalties), subclass Scorer and pass it to a custom engine.

import pandas as pd

# 1. Drop the nearly-empty encoded column
df_clean = df.drop(columns=["encoded_field"])

# 2. Drop the ID column (not useful for modelling)
df_clean = df_clean.drop(columns=["id"])

# 4. Impute minor missing values

docs.title: Example: Writing a Custom Plugin
docs.text: This example builds a complete custom plugin from scratch — a Phone Number Detector — and integrates it into NowEDA's engine with scoring and insights.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Detect phone numbers in string columns and add them to the risk score.

docs.title: Step 1 — Write the plugin
docs.text:
# my_plugins/phone.py

import re
from noweda.plugins.base import BasePlugin


class PhoneNumberPlugin(BasePlugin):
    """Detect phone number patterns in string columns."""

    name = "phone"

    # Matches formats like: +1-800-555-1234, (800) 555 1234, 8005551234
    PHONE_REGEX = re.compile(
        r"\b(\+?1[-.\s]?)?(\(?\d{3}\)?[-.\s]?)?\d{3}[-.\s]?\d{4}\b"
    )

    def run(self, df):
        findings = {}

        for col in df.columns:
            if df[col].dtype != "object":
                continue

            matches = (
                df[col]
                .astype(str)
                .str.contains(self.PHONE_REGEX, regex=True)
                .sum()
            )

            if matches > 0:
                findings[col] = {"phones_detected": int(matches)}

        return findings

docs.title: Step 2 — Use the plugin standalone
docs.text:
import pandas as pd
from my_plugins.phone import PhoneNumberPlugin

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "name":    ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol"],
    "contact": ["Call me at 800-555-1234", "No phone", "+1 (415) 555-9876"],
    "value":   [100, 200, 300],
})

plugin = PhoneNumberPlugin()
result = plugin.run(df)

print(result)
# {'contact': {'phones_detected': 2}}

docs.title: Step 3 — Add it to NowEDA's engine
docs.text:
import noweda as eda
from noweda.core.engine import AutoEDAEngine
from noweda.plugins import default_plugins
from my_plugins.phone import PhoneNumberPlugin

# Add phone plugin to the default stack
plugins = default_plugins() + [PhoneNumberPlugin()]
engine = AutoEDAEngine(plugins)

df = eda.read("contacts.csv")
report = engine.run_df(df)

print(report["results"]["phone"])
# {'contact': {'phones_detected': 2}}

docs.title: Step 4 — Make it affect the risk score
docs.text:
Subclass Scorer to include phone numbers in the risk calculation:

from noweda.scoring.scorer import Scorer


class ExtendedScorer(Scorer):
    def compute(self, results):
        scores = super().compute(results)   # run base scoring

        phone = results.get("phone", {})
        scores["risk"] += len(phone) * 10  # +10 per phone column

        return scores

docs.title: Step 5 — Make it generate insights
docs.text:
Subclass InsightGenerator to add phone-specific insights:

from noweda.insights.generator import InsightGenerator


class ExtendedInsightGenerator(InsightGenerator):
    def generate(self, results, scores):
        insights = super().generate(results, scores)

        phone = results.get("phone", {})
        for col, info in phone.items():
            insights.append(
                f"Phone numbers detected in column '{col}': "
                f"{info['phones_detected']} match(es). "
                f"Remove or mask before sharing."
            )

        return insights

docs.title: Step 6 — Wire everything into a custom engine
docs.text:
from noweda.core.engine import AutoEDAEngine


class SecureEngine(AutoEDAEngine):
    """Engine with phone detection, extended scoring, and custom insights."""

    def run_df(self, df):
        results = {}
        for plugin in self.plugins:
            results[plugin.name] = plugin.run(df)

        scores = ExtendedScorer().compute(results)
        insights = ExtendedInsightGenerator().generate(results, scores)

        return {"results": results, "scores": scores, "insights": insights}


# Use it
plugins = default_plugins() + [PhoneNumberPlugin()]
engine = SecureEngine(plugins)

df = eda.read("contacts.csv")
report = engine.run_df(df)

for insight in report["insights"]:
    print(insight)

docs.title: Step 7 — Integrate with df.noweda.*
docs.text:
To make your custom engine the default used by df.noweda.*, patch the accessor before any analysis runs:

import noweda.accessor as _accessor
from noweda.accessor import NowEDAAccessor


class SecureAccessor(NowEDAAccessor):
    def _ensure_analyzed(self):
        if self._report is None:
            plugins = default_plugins() + [PhoneNumberPlugin()]
            engine = SecureEngine(plugins)
            self._report = engine.run_df(self._df)


# Re-register the accessor
import pandas as pd
pd.api.extensions.register_dataframe_accessor("noweda")(SecureAccessor)

# Now df.noweda.* uses your custom engine
df = eda.read("contacts.csv")
df.noweda.insights()   # includes phone number insights

docs.title: Example: Working with Multiple Formats
docs.text: NowEDA reads 28 file extensions. This example shows how to load and analyse data from several different formats in one workflow.

docs.title: Loading Different Formats
docs.text:
import noweda as eda

# Delimited text
csv_df   = eda.read("sales.csv")
tsv_df   = eda.read("events.tsv")

# Spreadsheets
xlsx_df  = eda.read("report.xlsx", sheet_name="Summary")
ods_df   = eda.read("budget.ods")

# Structured data
json_df  = eda.read("api_response.json")
xml_df   = eda.read("records.xml")
html_df  = eda.read("web_table.html")

# Big data formats (requires: pip install "noweda[parquet]")
parquet_df = eda.read("transactions.parquet")
feather_df = eda.read("cache.feather")

# Statistical software
stata_df  = eda.read("survey.dta")
sas_df    = eda.read("results.sas7bdat")

# Python
pickle_df = eda.read("model_data.pkl")

docs.title: Scanning Multiple Files at Once
docs.text:
import os
import pandas as pd
import noweda as eda

def scan_directory(folder):
    """Run NowEDA on every supported file in a folder."""
    supported = {
        ".csv", ".tsv", ".xlsx", ".xls", ".json",
        ".xml", ".html", ".parquet", ".pkl", ".dta"
    }

    results = {}

    for fname in os.listdir(folder):
        ext = os.path.splitext(fname)[1].lower()
        if ext not in supported:
            continue

        path = os.path.join(folder, fname)
        try:
            df = eda.read(path)
            results[fname] = {
                "rows":         len(df),
                "columns":      len(df.columns),
                "scores":       df.noweda.score(),
                "top_insights": df.noweda.insights()[:3],
            }
        except Exception as e:
            results[fname] = {"error": str(e)}

    return results


findings = scan_directory("data/")

for filename, info in findings.items():
    if "error" in info:
        print(f"  {filename}: ERROR — {info['error']}")
    else:
        s = info["scores"]
        print(f"  {filename} ({info['rows']} rows): "
              f"quality={s['data_quality']}, risk={s['risk']}")

docs.title: Comparing Two Versions of the Same Dataset
docs.text:
A common use case: compare a new data export against last month's version.

import noweda as eda

old = eda.read("customers_march.csv")
new = eda.read("customers_april.csv")

old_scores = old.noweda.score()
new_scores = new.noweda.score()

print("Score comparison:")
for metric in ["data_quality", "risk", "model_readiness"]:
    delta = new_scores[metric] - old_scores[metric]
    direction = "↑" if delta > 0 else ("↓" if delta < 0 else "→")
    print(f"  {metric:20s}: {old_scores[metric]} → {new_scores[metric]}  {direction} {abs(delta)}")

Score comparison:
  data_quality        : 77 → 85  ↑ 8
  risk                : 25 → 10  ↓ 15
  model_readiness     : 53 → 71  ↑ 18

docs.title: Converting Formats
docs.text:
Load one format, transform, export another:

import noweda as eda

# Load Excel, inspect, save as Parquet (much faster for big data)
df = eda.read("large_report.xlsx")

print(df.noweda.score())

# Check it's clean before converting
if df.noweda.score()["data_quality"] >= 70:
    df.to_parquet("large_report.parquet", index=False)
    print("Saved as Parquet.")
else:
    print("Data quality too low — clean before converting.")
